merged_findings.finding: Phase 1 deps.py soft-check references `POST https://below.local:<port>/api/classify` but the dependency table establishes Below at `http://10.0.1.105:8100`. Both hostname (`below.local` vs IP literal) and protocol (`https` vs `http`) are inconsistent. If the implementation uses the literal `below.local` hostname it may fail to resolve on machines without that DNS/mDNS entry.

claude.finding: Phase 1 deps.py soft-check references `POST https://below.local:<port>/api/classify` but the dependency table establishes Below at `http://10.0.1.105:8100`. Both hostname (`below.local` vs IP literal) and protocol (`https` vs `http`) are inconsistent. If the implementation uses the literal `below.local` hostname it may fail to resolve on machines without that DNS/mDNS entry.

merged_findings.finding: Phase 1 creates `scripts/install-benchmark-deps.sh` and Phase 5 creates `scripts/install-benchmark-tools.sh`, but no `scripts/` directory exists in the project file tree. Neither phase mentions creating the parent directory. File creation will fail if `scripts/` is not mkdir'd first.

claude.finding: Phase 1 creates `scripts/install-benchmark-deps.sh` and Phase 5 creates `scripts/install-benchmark-tools.sh`, but no `scripts/` directory exists in the project file tree. Neither phase mentions creating the parent directory. File creation will fail if `scripts/` is not mkdir'd first.

merged_findings.finding: Phase 1's BenchmarkConfig schema exhaustively lists every config field across all 10 phases but omits cluster node URLs (Below `10.0.1.105:8100`, Walter `10.0.1.102:8100`, Lomax `10.0.1.104:8100`). Phase 2's `api_clients.py` and Phase 1's `deps.py` both need these URLs at runtime, but the plan never specifies whether they come from `config.yaml`, `~/.buildrunner/cluster.json`, or are hardcoded — leaving an undefined configuration surface.

claude.finding: Phase 1's BenchmarkConfig schema exhaustively lists every config field across all 10 phases but omits cluster node URLs (Below `10.0.1.105:8100`, Walter `10.0.1.102:8100`, Lomax `10.0.1.104:8100`). Phase 2's `api_clients.py` and Phase 1's `deps.py` both need these URLs at runtime, but the plan never specifies whether they come from `config.yaml`, `~/.buildrunner/cluster.json`, or are hardcoded — leaving an undefined configuration surface.

merged_findings.finding: Phases 3, 5, and 6 all MODIFY `runner.py`'s `run_cell()` method body sequentially: Phase 3 creates the base flow, Phase 5 inserts the scorer call inside try/finally, Phase 6 inserts judge + probe_scheduler calls after scoring. Three sequential insertions into the same function body across three phases is fragile — each later phase must understand the exact code layout left by the prior phase, and any deviation in Phase 3's implementation from what Phase 5/6 expect will cascade.

claude.finding: Phases 3, 5, and 6 all MODIFY `runner.py`'s `run_cell()` method body sequentially: Phase 3 creates the base flow, Phase 5 inserts the scorer call inside try/finally, Phase 6 inserts judge + probe_scheduler calls after scoring. Three sequential insertions into the same function body across three phases is fragile — each later phase must understand the exact code layout left by the prior phase, and any deviation in Phase 3's implementation from what Phase 5/6 expect will cascade.

merged_findings.finding: Phase 5 scorer invokes `.venv-benchmark/bin/pytest -q --tb=no -p no:cacheprovider` and explicitly omits `--no-cov` because pytest-cov is not in requirements-benchmark.txt. However, a contestant's diff can add `pytest-cov` to any `requirements*.txt` (which the runner installs before pytest), and pytest-cov auto-activates via its plugin hook. Without `-p no:pytest_cov`, coverage instrumentation would silently run, adding overhead and potentially altering exit behavior across contestant runs non-uniformly.

claude.finding: Phase 5 scorer invokes `.venv-benchmark/bin/pytest -q --tb=no -p no:cacheprovider` and explicitly omits `--no-cov` because pytest-cov is not in requirements-benchmark.txt. However, a contestant's diff can add `pytest-cov` to any `requirements*.txt` (which the runner installs before pytest), and pytest-cov auto-activates via its plugin hook. Without `-p no:pytest_cov`, coverage instrumentation would silently run, adding overhead and potentially altering exit behavior across contestant runs non-uniformly.

merged_findings.finding: Phase 6's `cli_run.py` MODIFY extends the Runner constructor to `Runner(scorer=BenchmarkScorer(), judge=BenchmarkJudge(), probe_scheduler=ProbeScheduler())`, but Phase 5's MODIFY of the same file set it to `Runner(scorer=BenchmarkScorer())`. The plan describes these as sequential edits, but the Phase 5 edit text is never shown — if Phase 5's edit uses a specific string pattern for the constructor call, Phase 6's Edit must match that exact pattern. A mismatch (e.g., multiline vs single-line formatting, trailing comma differences) would cause the Phase 6 edit to fail to find its replacement target.

claude.finding: Phase 6's `cli_run.py` MODIFY extends the Runner constructor to `Runner(scorer=BenchmarkScorer(), judge=BenchmarkJudge(), probe_scheduler=ProbeScheduler())`, but Phase 5's MODIFY of the same file set it to `Runner(scorer=BenchmarkScorer())`. The plan describes these as sequential edits, but the Phase 5 edit text is never shown — if Phase 5's edit uses a specific string pattern for the constructor call, Phase 6's Edit must match that exact pattern. A mismatch (e.g., multiline vs single-line formatting, trailing comma differences) would cause the Phase 6 edit to fail to find its replacement target.

merged_findings.finding: Phase 9's contention measurement filters CompletedRow by session_id, but CompletedRow.session_id is a field on the row itself (declared in Phase 1). The pilot invocation needs to pass the pilot's session_id to `pilot_contention.py --session-id <id>`. The plan says this comes 'from the pilot's Typer context' but doesn't specify how the Typer context captures and forwards the session_id that `br benchmark run --pilot` internally minted (a uuid4). The wiring from the Typer command body to the pilot_contention invocation is underdefined.

claude.finding: Phase 9's contention measurement filters CompletedRow by session_id, but CompletedRow.session_id is a field on the row itself (declared in Phase 1). The pilot invocation needs to pass the pilot's session_id to `pilot_contention.py --session-id <id>`. The plan says this comes 'from the pilot's Typer context' but doesn't specify how the Typer context captures and forwards the session_id that `br benchmark run --pilot` internally minted (a uuid4). The wiring from the Typer command body to the pilot_contention invocation is underdefined.

merged_findings.finding: The plan states `.buildrunner/benchmarks/config.yaml` is created as NEW in Phase 1, but the full BenchmarkConfig schema has ~50 fields with canonical default values scattered across the plan prose. No template or default-value generator is described — the operator must manually author the YAML with correct field names, nesting, and values. A single typo in config.yaml (e.g., `contention_ratio_theshold` instead of `contention_ratio_threshold`) would pass YAML parsing but fail pydantic validation at preflight. Consider shipping a validated default config alongside the schema.

claude.finding: The plan states `.buildrunner/benchmarks/config.yaml` is created as NEW in Phase 1, but the full BenchmarkConfig schema has ~50 fields with canonical default values scattered across the plan prose. No template or default-value generator is described — the operator must manually author the YAML with correct field names, nesting, and values. A single typo in config.yaml (e.g., `contention_ratio_theshold` instead of `contention_ratio_threshold`) would pass YAML parsing but fail pydantic validation at preflight. Consider shipping a validated default config alongside the schema.

merged_findings.finding: Phase 2's `lockres.resolve_lock` uses `git log --all --extended-regexp --grep='^lock: claim Phase N($|[^0-9])'`. The `--all` flag searches all refs including remote-tracking branches. The plan adds a Stage 1b reachability filter via `git merge-base --is-ancestor`, but this runs one subprocess per candidate SHA. In a repo with many remote branches and repeated phase numbers across BUILD files, this could spawn dozens of subprocesses per phase. With 30+ phases to resolve, the selector's wall-clock could be unexpectedly long.

claude.finding: Phase 2's `lockres.resolve_lock` uses `git log --all --extended-regexp --grep='^lock: claim Phase N($|[^0-9])'`. The `--all` flag searches all refs including remote-tracking branches. The plan adds a Stage 1b reachability filter via `git merge-base --is-ancestor`, but this runs one subprocess per candidate SHA. In a repo with many remote branches and repeated phase numbers across BUILD files, this could spawn dozens of subprocesses per phase. With 30+ phases to resolve, the selector's wall-clock could be unexpectedly long.

merged_findings.finding: Phase 5's Node install uses `npm ci --ignore-scripts` as the primary path with `npm install --ignore-scripts` as fallback. A contestant whose diff adds a native Node module (e.g., `sharp`, `bcrypt`) that requires a postinstall compilation step will get a broken `node_modules/` and their tests will fail with a missing-binary error. The plan treats this as 'scored as model failure' but does not distinguish between a contestant who legitimately uses a native dep vs one who made a mistake — both get `install_failed`, which could systematically penalize one model if it tends to reach for native deps.

claude.finding: Phase 5's Node install uses `npm ci --ignore-scripts` as the primary path with `npm install --ignore-scripts` as fallback. A contestant whose diff adds a native Node module (e.g., `sharp`, `bcrypt`) that requires a postinstall compilation step will get a broken `node_modules/` and their tests will fail with a missing-binary error. The plan treats this as 'scored as model failure' but does not distinguish between a contestant who legitimately uses a native dep vs one who made a mistake — both get `install_failed`, which could systematically penalize one model if it tends to reach for native deps.

merged_findings.finding: Phase 6 wires `Runner.run_cell()` to call `self.probe_scheduler.notify_pair_judged(pair_key)`, but `core/benchmark/probe.py` is only specified to expose `ProbeScheduler.schedule(...)` and `ProbeScheduler.run_pending()`. `notify_pair_judged` is absent from the plan's declared API surface.

codex.finding: Phase 6 wires `Runner.run_cell()` to call `self.probe_scheduler.notify_pair_judged(pair_key)`, but `core/benchmark/probe.py` is only specified to expose `ProbeScheduler.schedule(...)` and `ProbeScheduler.run_pending()`. `notify_pair_judged` is absent from the plan's declared API surface.

merged_findings.finding: Recognition-probe triggering is contradictory. Phase 4 says a `(phase_id, model)` probe may run only after every cell for the entire phase is `stage="judged"`, but Phase 6 changes the trigger to fire when one `pair_key`/replication becomes judged. Those conditions are incompatible, and the Phase 6 trigger can run probes before remaining replications or sub-studies finish, violating the non-priming requirement.

codex.finding: Recognition-probe triggering is contradictory. Phase 4 says a `(phase_id, model)` probe may run only after every cell for the entire phase is `stage="judged"`, but Phase 6 changes the trigger to fire when one `pair_key`/replication becomes judged. Those conditions are incompatible, and the Phase 6 trigger can run probes before remaining replications or sub-studies finish, violating the non-priming requirement.

merged_findings.finding: The judge data model does not line up with the registry model. Phase 1 defines `CompletedRow` as a run-scoped row appended three times per `run_id`, while Phase 6 produces one pairwise `JudgeVerdict` keyed by `pair_key` and says the runner appends a singular `stage="judged"` `CompletedRow`. The plan never specifies how both underlying runs get terminal judged rows, so one side can remain stuck at `stage="scored"`, breaking resume logic and downstream aggregation.

codex.finding: The judge data model does not line up with the registry model. Phase 1 defines `CompletedRow` as a run-scoped row appended three times per `run_id`, while Phase 6 produces one pairwise `JudgeVerdict` keyed by `pair_key` and says the runner appends a singular `stage="judged"` `CompletedRow`. The plan never specifies how both underlying runs get terminal judged rows, so one side can remain stuck at `stage="scored"`, breaking resume logic and downstream aggregation.

merged_findings.finding: Phase 10's `achieved_phases = min(30, selector.pool_survivors_after_paraphrase_equivalence + selector.replacement_phases_enqueued_by_redraw)` can overcount. `replacement_phases_enqueued_by_redraw` counts redraw reservations, not successful surviving replacement phases, so a replacement that later also fails paraphrase/equivalence still inflates achieved N and can misapply the degraded-sample decision rule.

codex.finding: Phase 10's `achieved_phases = min(30, selector.pool_survivors_after_paraphrase_equivalence + selector.replacement_phases_enqueued_by_redraw)` can overcount. `replacement_phases_enqueued_by_redraw` counts redraw reservations, not successful surviving replacement phases, so a replacement that later also fails paraphrase/equivalence still inflates achieved N and can misapply the degraded-sample decision rule.
